topic: mother's borscht recipe — fresh beets debate

turns.content: strange question. my mother is visiting this weekend and she wants to make borscht together. she insists that canned beets are an insult to the dish. is there actually a meaningful difference when cooking borscht with fresh beets versus canned or pickled

Your mother is right, and this is a hill worth dying on.

Fresh beets make a significant difference in borscht, and it's not just tradition:

**Fresh beets:**
- Earthy, complex flavor with natural sweetness that develops during cooking
- Better texture — they hold their shape and have a slight bite
- The color is deeper and more vibrant, that true ruby-crimson
- You control the seasoning entirely — no added vinegar or preservatives competing with the broth
- The beet greens can go into the pot too if they're fresh

**Canned beets:**
- Pre-cooked, so the texture is soft and uniform — mushy in the soup
- Often packed in salted water or vinegar, which throws off the flavor balance
- The color leaches into the packing liquid, so you get a duller result
- Convenience is the only real advantage

**Pickled beets:**
- These belong in a salad, not borscht. The vinegar dominates everything

The difference is especially noticeable in a traditional recipe where the beet flavor is the foundation, not just a component. If your mother's borscht is the kind where beets are the star — and if she's insisting on fresh, it clearly is — canned would genuinely diminish the dish.

Does she grate the beets or cube them?

she grates them. always has. she says the grating releases more flavor into the broth. i have eaten this borscht my entire life and never once questioned any step of the process. you do not question a russian woman about her borscht. this is a survival rule

That is a universal truth that transcends cuisine.

She's right about the grating too. Grated beets have more surface area exposed to the broth, so they release their color, sweetness, and earthiness much faster and more completely. Cubed beets give you distinct pieces in the soup but less integration with the liquid.

Grating is the traditional Moscow/central Russian approach. Ukrainian-style borscht sometimes uses larger cuts because the beet is more of a chunky vegetable in the bowl. Both are valid — but if your mother grates, she's making a broth-forward borscht where the liquid itself is the point.

**If you're cooking together, here are a few things that might come up:**
- Grate the beets last, right before they go in — they oxidize and lose color quickly
- A splash of vinegar or lemon juice added during cooking keeps the color bright. She probably already does this
- If she uses beef broth — bone broth specifically — the collagen rounds out the beet flavor beautifully
- Sour cream served separately, not stirred in. Stirring it in turns the whole pot pink

Is she the type who works from a written recipe, or is it all by feel?

by feel. entirely. i once tried to write it down. i stood in the kitchen with a notebook and asked her measurements. she said a handful of this. some of that. until it looks right. i got a page of descriptions that would not help anyone reproduce the dish. it was like trying to document a process that only exists in muscle memory

That's the problem with recipes that have been passed down by practice instead of paper. The knowledge is stored in her hands and her eyes, not in measurements. "Until it looks right" is a perfectly valid instruction if you have 40 years of calibration data.

**If you want to actually capture the recipe this time:**

Don't ask for measurements. Instead:
- Measure behind her. She adds a handful of salt — you weigh what a handful is after she adds it
- Film the process on your phone. Not her face if she'd object, just the pot and her hands
- Note the timing. When does she add the beets relative to the onions. How long does it simmer. When does she taste and adjust
- Pay attention to the corrections. The moments where she tastes and adds something — that's where the real recipe lives

The goal isn't to get a recipe that anyone could follow. It's to get a recipe that you could follow. You've tasted the correct result a hundred times. You just need the sequence and rough proportions to get yourself close enough that your own memory fills in the rest.

Think of it as reverse engineering a system you've only ever interacted with as a user.

reverse engineering my mothers borscht. she would find this funny. or insulting. possibly both. i will try the weighing approach. discreetly. if she catches me with a kitchen scale she will have questions

Covert measurement collection. You could run this as a social engineering exercise. Objective: extract recipe data without the target becoming aware of the operation.

But seriously — the discreet approach is smart. Some things are better preserved imperfectly than not at all. Even approximate measurements put you ahead of "a handful of this" when you're trying to make it yourself someday.

One more thing: if she makes the broth from scratch, note what cut of meat she uses and whether the bones are roasted first. That's usually the part people forget to document because it happens before the "real" cooking starts.

Enjoy the weekend. There are worse problems than being forced to eat homemade borscht.

actually while we are on the subject. she also makes pelmeni from scratch. the dough. the filling. everything. i have tried to make them on my own and they fall apart in the water every time. the dough tears or the seal does not hold

Pelmeni that fall apart in boiling water — the two most common causes:

**Dough issue:**
- The dough is too dry. Pelmeni dough needs to be soft and pliable, not stiff. If it cracks when you fold it, add more water (or an egg if her recipe uses one)
- It wasn't rested. After kneading, the dough needs at least 30 minutes under a towel. The gluten relaxes and becomes elastic enough to stretch without tearing
- Rolled too thin. For pelmeni you want about 2mm thickness. Thinner looks elegant but tears under the weight of the filling

**Seal issue:**
- The edges are wet or oily from the filling. The meat mixture releases moisture that prevents the dough from bonding to itself
- You're not pressing firmly enough. The seal needs to be pinched, not just folded together
- Try brushing the edge with a tiny bit of water before sealing — creates a paste-like bond

**The boiling itself:**
- Don't dump them into a rolling boil. Bring the water to a boil, then reduce to a gentle simmer before adding pelmeni
- Drop them in one at a time, not all at once. Crowding causes them to bump into each other and tear
- Stir gently once after adding them to prevent sticking to the bottom

Ask your mother what temperature her dough water is. That's usually where the home versions go wrong.

i never thought about the water temperature for the dough. i just use cold water. knowing her she probably uses warm. she has never specified. it is in the category of things she does without thinking about them

Warm water — not hot, about 40-45 degrees Celsius — makes the dough more elastic and easier to work with. Cold water produces a stiffer dough that's harder to seal. That single variable could be your entire problem.

This weekend, when she's making the borscht, ask her to show you the pelmeni too. Watch the water temperature, watch how she kneads, watch the thickness she rolls to. Same covert documentation approach — except this time she might actually be willing to teach it directly. Pelmeni is a task that traditionally gets passed down by standing next to someone and doing it together.

Or just measure the water temperature when she's not looking. Add it to the intelligence file.
the intelligence file on my mothers cooking. this is what my career has come to. conducting surveillance on borscht and pelmeni production. there are worse missions

Lower stakes. Better outcomes. And the target feeds you when the operation is complete.

One final note — if the pelmeni work out, make a big batch and freeze them. They freeze perfectly on a floured tray and then transfer to a bag. Having her pelmeni in the freezer for a random Tuesday night is worth the effort.
da. frozen pelmeni reserves. strategic food stockpile. i will do this. thank you for the cooking advice. this was not what i expected to need today but here we are
The best conversations are the unexpected ones. Enjoy the weekend. There are no problems. Only borscht and pelmeni.
